coredata.dc:title: Smartphone based platform for ratiometric fluorometric and colorimetric determination H2O2 and glucose
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Tao
Zhang, Shuxin
Liu, Wei
Zhao, Shun
Lu, Zhiwei
Wang, Yanying
Wang, Guangtu
Zou, Ping
Wang, Xianxiang
Zhao, Qingbiao
Rao, Hanbing
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a new strategy for ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric determination of H2O2 and glucose is proposed. The sensing system involves catalytic oxidation reaction of glucose oxidase (GOx) with glucose to generate H2O2. Under the catalysis of Fe2+, the 580 nm emission of carbon dots (C-dots) upon 365 nm excitation is reduced by H2O2, while the fluorescence emission at 450 nm of thiochrome (oxVB1) enhances with the oxidation reaction between H2O2/Fe2+system and VB1 (thiamine). Simultaneously, the color of the carbon dots (C-dots) suspension changes from purplish-red to colorless. Thus, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I450/I580) and the absorbance (A557) can be used to quantitatively determine H2O2 and glucose. Furthermore, we integrated fluorometric and colorimetric output with a smartphone platform to conduct on-site analysis of glucose without the need of spectrometer. A smartphone application (APP) was designed to take pictures and analyze R, G, B values of fluorometric and colorimetric signals, which delivered excellent analytical result for glucose. This ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric sensing system on a smartphone platform ensures sensitive and convenient determination of glucose and H2O2, hence having great prospects in biomedical analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescence
Colorimetry
Glucose
Smartphone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric determination of H2O2
Ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric determination of glucose
Analysis of glucose in real samples
The working mechanism of ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric sensing system
The feasibility of the sensing system
Sensitivity of the H2O2 ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric sensing system
Combination with glucose oxidase for the analysis of glucose
Selectivity and interference of the glucose
Application to the serum samples detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A)TEM images of prepared C-dots. The insert: HRTEM images of C-dots. (B) Zeta potential data of C-dots.
XPS spectrums of C-dots (A) XPS full spectrum. (B) High resolution C 1s, (C) N 1s and (D) O 1s spectrum.

The fluorescence emission spectrum (A) (a) C-dots only, (b) after the addition of Fe2+, (c) after the addition of H2O2, and(d) after the addition of Fe2+ and H2O2. (B) (e) VB1 only, (f) After the addition of Fe2+, (g) after the addition of H2O2, and (h) after the addition of Fe2+and H2O2. (C) (a) C-dots only, (b)after the addition of VB1, (c) after the addition of VB1 and Fe2+, (d) after the addition of VB1 and H2O2, and (e) after the addition of VB1/Fe2+/H2O2. The ultraviolet absorption spectra including (D) (a) C-dots only, (b) after the addition of Fe2+, (c) after the addition of H2O2, (d) after the addition of VB1, and (e) after the addition of Fe2+/H2O2/VB1. The data were measured with phosphate buffer solution (PB, 10 mM, pH = 2.4) under the excitation of 365 nm. The concentrations of Fe2+, VB1 and H2O2 were 180 μM, 30 μM and 120 μM, respectively.

(A) Ratio fluorescence emission spectra of C-dots/Fe2+/VB1 system with the H2O2 concentrations from 0 to 700 μM. Inset: the fluorescent image for H2O2 determination. (B) The linear relationship between I450/I580 and H2O2 concentrations from 0.5–500 μM. I450 and I580 represent the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm and 580 nm, respectively. (C) Absorption spectra of C-dots/Fe2+/VB1 system with the H2O2 concentrations from 0 to 700 μM. Inset: the colorimetric image for H2O2 detection. (D) The linear relationship between the absorption peak at 557 nm and H2O2 concentrations in the range of 0.5–200 μM.

(A) Ratiometric fluorescence spectra of Fe2+/VB1/GOx/C-dots mixed with the glucose in the 0–500 μM concentrations range. (B) The linear relation between I450/I580 and glucose concentrations in the range of 0.8–300 μM. I450 and I580 represent the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm and 580 nm, respectively. (C) Absorption spectra of C-dots/Fe2+/GOx/VB1 mixed with the glucose in the 0–500 μM concentrations range. (D) The linear relation between A557 and glucose in the 5–120 μM concentrations range. A557 represents the absorption intensity at 557 nm.

(A) The ratio of fluorescence intensity (I450/I580) and (B) the absorption peak at 557 nm in the presence of interferents without or with 100 μM glucose. The interferents include KCl (a), ZnCl2 (b), CuCl2 (c), AlCl3 (d), CdCl2 (e), NiCl2 (f), MgCl2 (g), NH4Cl (h), Fe(NO3) (i), Fe(NO3) (j), lysine (k), tryptophan (l), cysteine (m), histidine (n), arginine (o), glycine (p), glutathione (q), hyaluronic acid (r), sucrose (s), dopamine (t), ascorbic acid (u), fructose (v), and lactose (w).

(A) Integration of the fluorescent signal with a smartphone. (B) the linear relationship between (R + G+B)/3 and glucose concentrations from 20 to 100 μM (C) the average of R, G, B versus glucose concentrations from 5 to 360 μM.
The mechanism of H2O2 and glucose determination based on C-dots and VB1.
Comparison of determination methods for H2O2.
Comparison of determination methods for glucose.

head.ce:title: Smartphone based platform for ratiometric fluorometric and colorimetric determination H2O2 and glucose

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Information Engineering, Sichuan Agricultural University, Xinkang Road, Yucheng District, Ya’an, 625014, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Information Engineering, Sichuan Agricultural University, Xinkang Road, Yucheng District, Ya’an 625014, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Xin Kang Road, Yucheng District, Ya’an, 625014, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: 2College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Xin Kang Road, Yucheng District, Ya’an 625014, China, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Polar Materials and Devices (MOE), Department of Electronic Science, East China Normal University, Shanghai, 200241, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Polar Materials and Devices (MOE)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: 3Key Laboratory of Polar Materials and Devices (MOE), Department of Electronic Science, East China Normal University, Shanghai, 200241 P. R. China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors wish it to be known that, in their opinions, Tao Liu and Shuxin Zhang should be regarded as joint First Authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Glucose oxidase catalyzes glucose to produce H2O2, and Fe2+ catalyzes H2O2, generating hydroxyl radicals to oxidize vitamin B1 and statically quench C-dots fluorescence, accompanied by a color change from purplish-red to colorless. Furthermore, the fluorometric and colorimetric output was integrated with a smartphone to conduct on-site analysis of glucose without the need of spectrometer.

In this work, a new strategy for ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric determination of H2O2 and glucose is proposed. The sensing system involves catalytic oxidation reaction of glucose oxidase (GOx) with glucose to generate H2O2. Under the catalysis of Fe2+, the 580 nm emission of carbon dots (C-dots) upon 365 nm excitation is reduced by H2O2, while the fluorescence emission at 450 nm of thiochrome (oxVB1) enhances with the oxidation reaction between H2O2/Fe2+system and VB1 (thiamine). Simultaneously, the color of the carbon dots (C-dots) suspension changes from purplish-red to colorless. Thus, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I450/I580) and the absorbance (A557) can be used to quantitatively determine H2O2 and glucose. Furthermore, we integrated fluorometric and colorimetric output with a smartphone platform to conduct on-site analysis of glucose without the need of spectrometer. A smartphone application (APP) was designed to take pictures and analyze R, G, B values of fluorometric and colorimetric signals, which delivered excellent analytical result for glucose. This ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric sensing system on a smartphone platform ensures sensitive and convenient determination of glucose and H2O2, hence having great prospects in biomedical analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: As an important biological molecule, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is mainly produced in metabolism through the oxidative decomposition process [1]. Excessive H2O2 can cause abdominal pain, chest pain, dyspnea, vomiting, etc. The determination of H2O2 is essential in the treatment of diseases and related diagnostics. Simultaneously, H2O2 plays an important role in modern analytical science [2–6]. Glucose is a pivotal constituent of plant and animal carbohydrates in biological system. Glucose provides nutrients in metabolism of living organisms, and the heat released by its oxidation reaction is a major energy source for human life activities [7,8]. Moreover, glucose level is used as an indicator for the diagnosis of diabetes or hypoglycemia. Thus, it is critical to detect glucose rapidly and sensitively in food analysis and clinical diagnosis. A plethora of methods have been reported on the analysis of H2O2 and glucose, including chemiluminescence (CL) [9–11], Rayleigh scattering spectroscopy [12], electrochemical methods [13–15], fluorescence [16–20], electrochemiluminescence (ECL) [21], spectrophotometry [22–25], etc. Among these analytical techniques, fluorometry and spectrophotometry are widely used because of the rapidity, simplicity and sensitivity. Most reported methods are based on single signal determination, for instance, Chang et al. designed a fluorometric determination method for H2O2 and glucose based on TPE-M-L [26][Wang, 2019 #24;Zhai, #77]. Wang et al. used MoS2 QDS for fluorescent determination of H2O2 and glucose [27]. These methods were easily disturbed by changes in the instruments, parameters and external environments. Oppositely, ratiometric fluorescence can avoid external interference due to its self-correction function. Moreover, the ratiometric fluorescence sensing system can exhibit multiple fluorescent color changes for visualization. Hence, we designed the colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence dual-signal sensing system for determination of H2O2 and glucose.

C-dots have the advantages of low toxicity, low cost, high fluorescence intensity, good biocompatibility, stable chemical properties and convenient preparation. C-dots have been used as a probe to determine analytes such as metal ions [28], small organic molecules [29] and biological macromolecules [30]. However, C-dots typically exhibit blue-green fluorescence, and the fluorescence C-dots with emission in long wavelengths were rarely reported. Therefore, preparation of red-emissive fluorescence C-dots is in demand.

With the development of information technology, smartphones have become extremely accessible. Recent studies have proposed using smartphone as detectors for colorimetric and fluorescence measurements [31–34]. This approach not only reduces the cost of time and expense, but also enables on-demand analysis and on-site testing.

Herein, we proposed a convenient, efficient and label-free ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric sensing system for H2O2 and glucose. Firstly, glucose oxidase catalyzes the glucose to generate H2O2. After that, hydroxyl radical (·OH) is produced via Fenton reaction between Fe2+ and H2O2 in acidic condition. Finally, the red fluorescence of C-dots is quenched by ·OH. Simultaneously, the ·OH can oxidize non-fluorescent VB1 to produce blue-emitting oxVB1. Moreover, the UV absorption peak of C-dots at 557 nm is significantly reduced, and the color of C-dots fades with increasing H2O2 concentrations. Therefore, a novel ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric strategy is established for H2O2 and glucose determination. Furthermore, a smartphone-based platform was designed for on-site analysis of glucose. The fluorescent and colorimetric images are collected by a smartphone when various glucose concentrations are added, and these images are recognized and computed with R, G, B values by our self-developed APP for smartphone to quantitatively analyze glucose concentrations.

The reagents of KCl, ZnCl2, CuCl2, AlCl3, CdCl2, NiCl2, MgCl2, NH4Cl, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %), Fe(NO3)3 and Fe(NO3)2 were obtained from Chengdu KeLong. (Chengdu, China). Lysine, tryptophan, cystine, histidine, arginine, glycine, glutathione, hyaluronic acid, sucrose, dopamine, ascorbic acid, fructose, lactose, thiamine (VB1), glucose, glucose oxidase and Rhodamine B were manufactured by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Buffers with different pH were made with 20 mM KH2PO4-K2HPO4. All reagents are of analytical grade without further processing before use. The deionized water was employed as solvent throughout experimental process. Clinical serum samples were supplied by Chengdu First People's Hospital (Chengdu, China).

The fluorescence spectra were displayed on a fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer instrument Co. Ltd., Llantrisant, UK). The absorption spectra of C-dots were performed on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Cary 60, USA). The zeta potential data was collected with a Malvern Zetasizer apparatus (Zetasizer WT, UK). Time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer (PLS 980, Edinburgh, UK) was used to record fluorescence lifetime. The AFM images of C-dots were collected with an atomic force microscope (Bruker dimension icon, Germany). FTIR spectrum was collected with a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR-8400S, Shimadzu, Japan). TEM images of C-dots were recorded by JEM 2100 high resolution TEM (JEOL 2100 F, Japan). The element composition and valence states of C-dots were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (AMICUS, Shimadzu, Japan). The glucose concentrations in serum samples were tested with a Beckman Kurt Biochemical Instrument (AU5800, USA). Photographs were taken, identified and calculated by vivo x9s.

The red-emissive C-dots were synthesized with certain modifications based on a previously reported method [35]. Specifically, 0.25 g Rhodamine B was added to 10.00 ml ultrapure water and fully dissolved under ultrasonication for 15 min. The solution was heated in autoclave at 160 ℃ for 2 h. After cooling down, the obtained solution was centrifuged at 11,000 rpm for 20 min to remove insoluble particulate matter. The supernatant was added to a 1000 molecular weight cut off (MWCO) dialysis bag and placed in ultrapure water overnight. The resulting C-dots suspension was freeze-dried and preserved at -4 ℃.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric determination of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: 200 μL of 6 mM C-dots aqueous solution and 2 mL phosphate buffer solution (PB, 10 mM, pH = 2.4) were mixed with different concentrations of H2O2 (200 μL), and then 200 μL of 0.45 mM VB1 was added into the mixed solution. 200 μL of 2.7 mM Fe(NO3)2 was rapidly mixed into the obtained solution. The reaction was permitted to proceed for 10 min at 30 ℃, and the resulting solution was mixed with 200 μL of ultrapure water. Finally, fluorometric and colorimetric spectra were recorded. The whole experiment operation was repeated for three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric determination of glucose
ce:section.ce:para: A typical process for glucose determination was as follows: 200 μL of GOx (0.5 μg/mL) was incubated with diverse glucose concentrations (200 μL) in PB (10 mM, pH = 7.2) at 37 ℃ for 40 min to produce H2O2, the remaining steps were the same as the determination of H2O2.

The mixed solution (C-dots/Fe2+/GOx/glucose) was placed into a black box and lighted with an ultraviolet lamp. The lamp was fastened on the side of the box to ensure uniform illumination. The smartphone was fixed at a distance of 10 cm from the fluorescent system. The fluorescent photographs were collected by a smartphone. The obtained images were cropped, and R, G, B values were read by our self-developed APP. Finally, the relationship between average values of R, G, B and glucose concentrations was fitted by the APP, and the linear relational equation and R2 value were displayed. For the colorimetric sensing by the smartphone, the mixed solution (C-dots/Fe2+/GOx/glucose) was placed into a black box and lighted with daylight lamp. The remaining steps were the same as the smartphone-based fluorescent determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of glucose in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Human serum samples were supplied by Chengdu First People's Hospital. Before the analysis, the serum samples were centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatants were collected and diluted by 150 times, 250 times and 750 times with PB (10 mM, pH = 7.2), respectively. The glucose concentrations in the pretreated samples were determined by the hexokinase method, and then the samples were spiked with glucose at various concentrations. Finally, the spiked samples were analyzed by the designed method to examine the recovery rates.

C-dots were prepared through hydrothermal carbonization, filtration and dialysis of Rhodamine B. As a fluorescent probe, the synthesized C-dots possessed more stable optical properties than raw material Rhodamine B [35]. TEM images confirmed that the C-dots were successfully synthesized with good dispersion (Fig. 1 A). HRTEM images (inset in Fig. 1A) clearly show the crystalline characteristics of C-dots with lattice fringe spacing of 0.21 nm. The zeta potential was measured as +1.85 mV, demonstrating that the C-dots are positively charged (Fig. 1B). The AFM images are shown in Fig. S1, indicating that the C-dots are uniformly distributed with a height of approximately 2.0 nm. The optical performances of C-dots were obtained by fluorescent and UV–vis spectrophotometer (Fig. S2). The C-dots exhibit a 580 nm fluorescence emission upon the excitation of 365 nm, with an absorption peak at 557 nm in the UV–vis spectrum. The relative fluorescence quantum efficiency of C-dots is 52 % based on Han's method [36], significantly higher than that of C-dots produced with other method [37]. The high quantum yield was attributed to the electron-rich oxygen and nitrogen surface groups of C-dots as excellent auxiliary chromophores.

The surface chemical characteristics of C-dots were measured by XPS. The survey spectrum (Fig. 2 A) displays that three elemental peaks at 525.1, 399.1 and 285.1 eV originate from O 1s, N 1s and C 1s, respectively. The peaks at 288.7, 288.0, 286.6, 285.8, 284.7 eV in the spectrum of C 1s are owing to the −COOH, CO, COC, CN and CC/CC bonds, respectively (Fig. 2B). The three peaks at 399.4, 401.4 and 406.2 eV on N 1s spectrum are owing to CNC, (C)3N and NO bonds (Fig. 2C). Two peaks at 531.6 and 533.1 eV in O1s spectrum originate from OC and OC (Fig. 2D). The XPS results confirm that the C-dots are composed of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. The results can be verified by the FTIR spectrum (Fig. S3). The stretching vibration of the OH functional group is manifested by a broad absorption band at 3448 cm−1. Absorption peak at 2962 cm−1 is owing to the CH bands. Absorption peaks at 2341 cm−1 and 1092 cm−1 are from CN bands. The absorption at 1627 cm−1 as a result of CC/CO stretching vibration. In addition, the minor absorption bands at 1450 cm−1 and 1230 cm−1 originate from CC and CO bands, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The working mechanism of ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Scheme 1 : (1) GOx catalytically oxidizes glucose to produce H2O2 and gluconic acid. (2) Fe2+ is converted into Fe3+ and ·OH by H2O2 through Fenton reaction in acidic solution. (3) ·OH oxidizes non-fluorescent substance VB1 to produce oxVB1 with an emission peak at 450 nm [38], and reduces the fluorescence peak of C-dots by static quenching, accompanied by a visible color change from purplish-red to colorless. Therefore, the fluorescent emission peak of oxVB1 at 450 nm and C-dots at 580 nm can be obtained by the excitation of 365 nm. With H2O2 as the intermediary, this C-dots-based ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric strategy is capable of quantitative determination of glucose. To prove the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime of C-dots was compared with that of Fe2+/H2O2/C-dots (Fig. S4). The lifetime had little change from 1.54 to 1.70 ns, which indicated that the quenching was static rather than dynamic [39,40]. Moreover, the fluorescence peak of C-dots was significantly reduced by the quencher, and the absorbance of C-dots also changed significantly. Based on the constant fluorescence lifetime and the change in the UV absorption peak of the C-dots, the fluorescence quenching could be due to the formation of a non-luminescent composite between the C-dots and Fe2+/H2O2, and the mechanism is static quenching. The static quenching of C-dots was accompanied by significant changes in the color of the solution, the absorbance at 557 nm was significantly reduced. Therefore, the H2O2 and glucose concentrations can be quantitatively determined by colorimetry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The feasibility of the sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: To further prove the feasibility of the sensing system using H2O2 as the intermediary, a series of control experiments were performed with the fluorescent spectrometer. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the fluorescent emissions of C-dots/Fe2+(b) and C-dots/H2O2(c) at 580 nm upon excitation of 365 nm had little change compared to C-dots alone(a), while the fluorescent emission of C-dots/Fe2+/H2O2(d) changed significanly. These results confimed that the quenching of C-dots was due to the Fenton reaction between Fe2+ and H2O2. As shown in Fig. 3B, while VB1/Fe2+/H2O2 solution exthibits the emission peak at 450 nm upon 365 nm excitation, the VB1(e), VB1/Fe2+(f) and VB1/H2O2(g) solutions did not exthibit fluorescent emission. It revealed that the VB1 itself does not emit fluorescence under UV light and the reaction products of Fe2+ and H2O2 can oxidize VB1 producing oxVB1. As the fluorescent emisssion at 450 nm enhances while the emission at 580 nm decreases, a ratiometric fluorescent signal can be established. In order to prove this, the C-dots were added into VB1(b), VB1/Fe2+(c) and VB1/H2O2(d) system, respectively (Fig. 3C). the fluorescent intensities of b, c and d were identical to that of C-dots (a). The C-dots and VB1 were mixed into the Fenton’s reagent at the same time, the decrease/increase of fluorescence for H2O2 occurred as expected(e). Furthermore, absorbance data were measured by using the UV–vis spectrophotometer. As shown in Fig. 3D, the absorbance of the C-dots at 557 nm did not weaken after VB1, Fe2+ and H2O2 were added separately. However, the absorption peak was significantly reduced as these components coexisted in the C-dots solution. In a word, these results verified the feasibility of ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric determination for H2O2 and glucose.

To achieve the optimal results with H2O2 as the intermediary, important experimental parameters, including Fe2+ concentration, reaction time, temperature, VB1 concentration and pH were optimized. As shown in Fig. S5A, with the Fe2+ concentration increased, the fluorescence ratio of I450/I580(C-dots/H2O2/VB1 system) raised rapidly and remained stable as the concentration reached 180 μM. The influence of reaction time in this sensing system was also investigated, as shown in Fig. S5B, the ratio of I450/I580(C-dots/Fe2+/H2O2/VB1 system) remained essentially constant after 10 min. As for temperature, different temperatures were tested for the C-dots/Fe2+/H2O2/VB1 system, and the ratio of I450/I580 reached maximum at 30℃ (Fig. S5C). As shown in Fig. S5D, the optimal concentration of VB1 was 30 μM. Moreover, different pH values in buffer solution were adjusted by 10 mM PB, and the highest value of I450/I580 was obtained at pH 2.4 (Fig. S5E). Therefore, Fe2+at 180 μM, incubation temperature of 30℃, pH = 2.4, VB1 at 30 μM and reaction time of 10 min were deemed the optimal experimental conditions and applied to subsequent analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of the H2O2 ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric sensing system
Combination with glucose oxidase for the analysis of glucose
Selectivity and interference of the glucose
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimized conditions, the selectivity of the C-dots-based fluorescent sensing system for glucose was confirmed by comparing with common interfering substances, including KCl, ZnCl2, CuCl2, AlCl3, CdCl2, NiCl2, MgCl2, NH4Cl, Fel(NO3), Fel(NO3), lysine, tryptophan, cysteine, histidine, arginine, glycine, glutathione, hyaluronic acid, sucrose, dopamine, ascorbic acid, fructose, lactose. Fig. 6 A shows the ratios of I450/I580 for the fluorescent sensing system incubated with glucose or other interfering substances. The concentration of the interferents were 100 times that of glucose. There is a sharp rise for the fluorescent intensity ratio (I450/I580) of glucose, whereas no obvious change was observed for other interfering substances. The I450/I580 of Fe2+/VB1/GOx/C-dots system incubated with solely glucose was close to that of the mixture of glucose and interfering substances. Additionally, Fig. 6B shows that the absorbance of the C-dots/Fe2+/VB1/GOx system at 557 nm incubated with glucose was significantly reduced, while it barely changed by incubating with the interfering substances. The colorimetric sensing system was added with solely glucose or with the mixture of glucose and other interferents, and the interfering substances did not affect the detection of glucose. Therefore, this method has high selectivity for glucose owing to the specific recognition of glucose based on glucose oxidase.

Instruments such as fluorescent spectrometer and UV–vis spectrophotometer are typically needed for fluorescent and colorimetric quantitative analysis, and the procedure is limited to laboratory operations. It is essential to develop a fast and convenient method to satisfy the requirement of on-site analysis. In this study, the fluorescent and colorimetric sensing system is integrated with a smartphone platform we developed. As shown in Fig. 7 A, as a series of concentrations of glucose were added to the C-dots/Fe2+/VB1/GOx system, the fluorescent images were collected by the smartphone. Then, these images were cropped and recorded by using an APP we developed, which can output R, G, B values. Finally, the correlation between (R + G+B)/3 value and glucose concentrations was calculated and fitted by the APP. Fig. 7B shows that the (R + G+B)/3 value is linearly related with glucose in the 20–100 μM concentrations range and the equation is (R + G+B)/3 = 1.062Cglucose + 61.36 (R2 = 0.9952). Fig. S7 show the process of smartphone-based colorimetric method, which is similar to the smartphone-based fluorescent method discussed above. Fig. 7C shows that the (R + G+B)/3 value is linearly related with glucose in the 5–360 μM concentrations range and the equation is (R + G+B)/3 = -0.1353Cglucose + 70.32 (R2 = 0.9966). Therefore, the results demonstrated that the strategy can be applied to the on-site analysis of glucose without the need of fluorescent spectrometer or UV–vis spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application to the serum samples detection
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the practicability of this sensing system, the results for determining glucose concentrations in human serums were compared with the hexokinase method, and the accuracy of the method was evaluated by the recovery of spiked samples. As shown in Table S1, the measured recovery rates were in the ranges of 96.97–105.00 % and 94.70–105.20 % for fluorescent and colorimetric sensing, respectively. Hence, the sensing system can quantitatively determine glucose levels in human serum.

To assess the practicality of the fluorescent and colorimetric sensing system with the smartphone platform for on-site analysis of glucose, glucose concentrations were firstly measured by the hexokinase method, then different concentrations of glucose were added to the human serum samples by standard addition method and analyzed by the APP (Fig. S8 and Fig. S9). The results from the APP are close to that from the hexokinase method. As shown in Table S2, the recovery rates were in the ranges of 97.30–102.80 % and 95.61–104.58 % for fluorescent and colorimetric sensing, respectively. Hence, the designed method can be applied to on-site analysis and determination of glucose in real samples.

A new strategy for ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric determination of glucose with high sensitivity was established. Glucose oxidase catalyzed glucose to product H2O2, and Fe2+ was used to catalyze H2O2, while the resulting hydroxyl radicals were capable of oxidizing VB1 and statically quenching C-dots to reach the purpose of determining glucose. The sensing system delivers high sensitivity with detection limits of 0.14 μM in the 0.8−300 μM concentrations range by fluorescent spectroscopy and 3.26 μM in the 5−120 μM concentrations range by UV–vis spectroscopy. This sensing system has high selectivity for determining glucose by fluorescent spectrometry and UV–vis. Hence, with H2O2 as the intermediary, this method can be availably applied to the quantitative analysis for H2O2 and glucose. Moreover, the C-dots-based fluorescent and colorimetric sensing system was integrated with a smartphone platform we designed. The smartphone platform is suitable for on-site analysis without the need of instruments. The results of glucose determination in real samples demonstrated the practicality of this fluorescent and colorimetric sensing system.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by grants from the Two-Way Support Programs of Sichuan Agricultural University (Grant Nos.1921993062, 1921993065) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21905186).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.127524.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel online fluorescence method for in-vivo measurement of hydrogen peroxide during oxidative stress produced in a temporal lobe epilepsy model

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Pardo-Peña, A. Sánchez-Lira, J.C. Salazar-Sánchez, A. Morales-Villagrán, A novel online fluorescence method for in-vivo measurement of hydrogen peroxide during oxidative stress produced in a temporal lobe epilepsy model, Neuroreport, 29 (2018) 621-630.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bifunctional MIL-53(Fe) with pyrophosphate-mediated peroxidase-like activity and oxidation-stimulated fluorescence switching for alkaline phosphatase detection

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A boronic acid based glucose assay based on the suppression of the inner filter effect of gold nanoparticles on the orange fluorescence of graphene oxide quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Weidan, Liu, Hua, Wang, Mingyue, Su, Xingguang, A boronic acid based glucose assay based on the suppression of the inner filter effect of gold nanoparticles on the orange fluorescence of graphene oxide quantum dots, Microchimica Acta, 184 (2017) 1463-1470.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Tao Liu is a professor in College of Information Engineering, Sichuan Agricultural University. He current interests is software development and application.
Shuxin Zhang is postgraduate student of College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University. His current interests include the development of biosensors and fluorescence sensors.
Wei Liu is postgraduate student of College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University. His current interests include the development of biosensors and fluorescence sensors.
Shun Zhao is postgraduate student of Information Engineering, Sichuan Agricultural University. He current interests is software development and application.
Zhiwei Lu is an associate professor of College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University. His current interests are modified electrode and bioelectroanalysis.

Yanying Wang received doctor degree in College of Chemistry, Sichuan University in 2011. Now she is an associate professor of College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University. Her current interests are modified electrode and bioelectroanalysis.
Guangtu Wang is an associate professor of College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University. He current interests are modified electrode and bioelectroanalysis.
Ping Zou is a professor in College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University. His current research interests include the isolation of phytochemicals and the synthesis of natural products.
Xianxiang Wang is a professor in College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University. current interests include the development of biosensors and fluorescence sensors.
Qingbiao Zhao is a professor in East China Normal University, he is a researcher in material science, information storage materials and strongly correlated materials.

Hanbing Rao received doctor degree in College of Chemistry, Sichuan University in 2010. Now he is an associate Professor of College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University. His current research interests are the preparation and the bioanalytical applications of nanomaterials.
